items.title: Silent model changes break clinical assurance
items.body: The detail that lit the fire was 'silent nerfing' — the model you validated may quietly become a different model. For an AI scribe that is a governance problem, not a curiosity: if the underlying model changes without notice, your local testing no longer guarantees how it behaves. Ask vendors for version pinning and change notification. As the episode put it, benchmarks assume the model you tested is the model you get — and in clinical use that assumption has to hold.

items.title: Data retention is the line that matters most for the NHS
items.body: Anthropic's thirty-day data-retention demand is the thread to pull. Whatever you make of the politics, any tool retaining identifiable consultation content raises an information-governance question for the practice, not just the supplier. Confirm where UK patient data is processed and stored, the retention window, and that a DPIA is in place. If Zitron is right that the labs are financially squeezed, expect pressure to retain or monetise data; if NLW is right that they hold unusual power, the concentration itself is the risk. The same precaution answers both.

items.title: Concentration is the real story — keep your options open
items.body: The 'power' debate is really about concentration in one provider. The practical response is ordinary procurement discipline: prefer tooling that is portable across models, with clear exit rights, so that one provider's abrupt policy swing cannot strand your clinic.
